title: 1 Kings 1
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — 1 Kings
paragraphs: 1 King David was now very old. They covered him with blankets, but he could not keep warm.
2 His servants said to him, "Let us find a young woman for our king. She will care for him and lie beside him to keep him warm."
3 So they searched all over Israel for a beautiful girl. They found Abishag from Shunem and brought her to the king.
4 The girl was very beautiful. She cared for the king and served him. But the king did not sleep with her.
5 At that time Adonijah, David's son by Haggith, began to lift himself up. He said, "I will be king!" He got chariots, riders, and fifty men to run ahead of him.
6 His father had never scolded him or asked, "Why do you act this way?" Adonijah was also very handsome. He was born next after Absalom.
7 Adonijah talked with Joab son of Zeruiah and with Abiathar the priest. Both men supported him.
8 But Zadok the priest, Benaiah son of Jehoiada, Nathan the prophet, Shimei, Rei, and David's strong soldiers did not join Adonijah.
9 Adonijah killed sheep, bulls, and fat young cows near the stone of Zoheleth, beside En-rogel. He invited all his royal brothers and all the men of Judah who served the king.
10 But he did not invite Nathan the prophet, Benaiah, the strong soldiers, or his brother Solomon.
11 Then Nathan spoke to Bathsheba, the mother of Solomon. He said, "Have you not heard? Adonijah son of Haggith has become king, and our lord David does not know it.
12 Now please let me give you advice. Save your own life and the life of your son Solomon.

13 Go at once to King David. Say to him, 'My lord the king, did you not promise your servant girl, "Your son Solomon will be king after me, and he will sit on my throne"? Why then has Adonijah become king?'
14 While you are still speaking with the king, I will come in after you. I will confirm your words."
15 So Bathsheba went to see the king in his bedroom. The king was very old, and Abishag from Shunem was serving him.
16 Bathsheba bowed low to the king. The king asked, "What do you want?"
17 "My lord," she replied, "you yourself promised your servant girl by the LORD your God. You said, 'Your son Solomon will be king after me, and he will sit on my throne.'
18 But now, look, Adonijah has become king. And you, my lord the king, do not know it.

19 He has killed many bulls, fat young cows, and sheep. He has invited all the other sons of the king. He has invited Abiathar the priest and Joab the army commander. But he did not invite your servant Solomon.
20 And now, my lord the king, all Israel is watching you. They want to know who will sit on your throne after you.
21 If you do not act, my son Solomon and I will be treated as criminals when you die."
22 While Bathsheba was still speaking with the king, Nathan the prophet arrived.
23 Someone told the king, "Nathan the prophet is here." Nathan went in and bowed his face to the ground before the king.
24 Nathan said, "My lord the king, did you say, 'Adonijah will be king after me, and he will sit on my throne'?

25 For today he has gone down and killed many bulls, fat young cows, and sheep. He has invited all the sons of the king, the army commanders, and Abiathar the priest. And look, they are eating and drinking before him. They are saying, 'Long live King Adonijah!'
26 But he did not invite me, your servant. He did not invite Zadok the priest, Benaiah son of Jehoiada, or your servant Solomon.
27 Has my lord the king done this without telling your servants who should sit on the throne after him?"
28 Then King David said, "Call Bathsheba for me." So she came in and stood before the king.
29 The king made a solemn promise. He said, "As surely as the LORD lives, who has saved my life from all trouble,
30 today I will do exactly what I promised you by the LORD, the God of Israel. Your son Solomon will be king after me. He will sit on my throne in my place."
31 Bathsheba bowed her face to the ground before the king. She said, "May my lord King David live forever!"
32 Then King David said, "Call Zadok the priest, Nathan the prophet, and Benaiah son of Jehoiada." So they came before the king.
33 The king said to them, "Take my servants with you. Set my son Solomon on my own mule. Take him down to Gihon.
34 There Zadok the priest and Nathan the prophet will pour oil on him to make him king over Israel. Blow the trumpet and shout, 'Long live King Solomon!'
35 Then come back up with him. He will sit on my throne and rule in my place. I have chosen him to be ruler over Israel and Judah."
36 Benaiah son of Jehoiada replied, "Amen! May the LORD, the God of my lord the king, also say so.
37 The LORD was with my lord the king. May He also be with Solomon. May He make Solomon's throne even greater than the throne of my lord King David."

38 So Zadok the priest, Nathan the prophet, and Benaiah son of Jehoiada went down. The king's guards, the Cherethites and Pelethites, went with them. They set Solomon on King David's mule and took him to Gihon.
39 Zadok the priest took the horn of oil from the holy tent. He poured oil on Solomon to make him king. Then they blew the trumpet, and all the people shouted, "Long live King Solomon!"
40 All the people followed him. They played flutes and rejoiced with such great joy that the ground shook with the sound.
41 Adonijah and all his guests were just finishing their feast. They heard the sound of the trumpet. Joab asked, "Why is the city so loud?"
42 While he was speaking, Jonathan son of Abiathar the priest arrived. Adonijah said, "Come in. You are a brave man. You must bring good news."
43 "No," Jonathan replied. "Our lord King David has made Solomon king.
44 The king sent Zadok the priest, Nathan the prophet, and Benaiah son of Jehoiada with Solomon. The Cherethites and Pelethites also went with him. They set him on the king's mule.
45 Zadok the priest and Nathan the prophet poured oil on him at Gihon to make him king. They have come up from there with great joy. That is the noise you hear.
46 Also, Solomon has taken his seat on the royal throne.

47 The king's servants have gone to bless our lord King David. They said, 'May your God make the name of Solomon greater than your name. May He make his throne greater than your throne.' Then the king bowed in worship on his bed.
48 The king said, 'Blessed be the LORD, the God of Israel! Today He has given me a son to sit on my throne, and my own eyes have seen it.'"
49 When they heard this, all the guests of Adonijah stood up in fear and ran away.
50 Adonijah was afraid of Solomon. He got up and went to hold on to the corners of the altar.

51 Someone told Solomon, "Look, Adonijah is afraid of King Solomon. He is holding on to the corners of the altar. He says, 'Let King Solomon first promise me that he will not kill his servant with the sword.'"
52 Solomon answered, "If he is a good man, not even one hair of his head will fall to the ground. But if evil is found in him, he will die."
53 So King Solomon sent for Adonijah, and they brought him down from the altar. He came and bowed before King Solomon. Solomon said to him, "Go to your home."

title: 1 Kings 2
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — 1 Kings
paragraphs: 1 When the time came near for David to die, he gave orders to his son Solomon.
2 He said, "I am about to die like all people on earth. So be strong and show that you are a man.
3 Obey what the LORD your God has said. Walk in His ways. Keep His laws, commands, rules, and orders, as written in the Law of Moses. Then you will do well in all you do.
4 Then the LORD will keep His promise to me. He said, 'If your sons walk faithfully before Me with all their heart and soul, a man from your family will always sit on the throne of Israel.'

5 You also know what Joab son of Zeruiah did to me. He killed Abner son of Ner and Amasa son of Jether, two leaders of Israel's armies. He killed them in peacetime to pay back the blood of war. He put the blood of war on his belt and his sandals.
6 So act wisely. Do not let his gray head go down to Sheol in peace.
7 But show kind love to the sons of Barzillai the Gileadite. Let them eat at your table. They stood by me when I fled from your brother Absalom.

8 Watch Shimei son of Gera, the Benjamite from Bahurim, who is with you. He spoke harsh curses against me on the day I went to Mahanaim. But when he came to meet me at the Jordan, I made a promise to him by the LORD: 'I will not kill you with the sword.'
9 Now do not treat him as innocent. You are a wise man. You know what you should do to bring his gray head down to Sheol in blood."
10 Then David died and joined his ancestors. He was buried in the City of David.
11 David ruled over Israel for forty years — seven years in Hebron and thirty-three years in Jerusalem.
12 So Solomon sat on the throne of his father David. And his kingdom became strong and secure.
13 Now Adonijah son of Haggith went to Bathsheba, the mother of Solomon. She asked, "Do you come in peace?" "Yes, in peace," he replied.
14 Then he said, "I have something to tell you." "Say it," she answered.
15 "You know that the kingship was mine," he said. "All Israel expected me to reign. But the kingship went to my brother. It came to him from the LORD.
16 So now I have one request of you. Do not say no to me." "Tell me what you want," she said.
17 Adonijah replied, "Please speak to King Solomon for me. He will not say no to you. Let him give me Abishag the Shunammite as my wife."
18 "Very well," Bathsheba replied. "I will speak to the king for you."

19 So Bathsheba went to King Solomon to speak to him for Adonijah. The king stood up to greet her and bowed to her. Then he sat down on his throne. He had a throne brought for his mother. She sat at his right hand.
20 "I have one small request of you," she said. "Do not say no to me." "Ask, my mother," the king replied. "I will not say no to you."
21 So Bathsheba said, "Let Abishag the Shunammite be given to your brother Adonijah as his wife."

22 King Solomon answered his mother, "Why do you ask for Abishag the Shunammite for Adonijah? He is my older brother. You might as well ask the kingdom for him, for Abiathar the priest, and for Joab son of Zeruiah!"
23 Then King Solomon made an oath by the LORD. He said, "May God punish me very much if Adonijah does not pay for this request with his life.
24 As the LORD lives — the One who set me firmly on the throne of my father David, and who gave me a royal family line as He promised — Adonijah will die today!"
25 So King Solomon sent the order to Benaiah son of Jehoiada. Benaiah struck down Adonijah, and he died.

26 Then the king said to Abiathar the priest, "Go back to your fields in Anathoth. You deserve to die. But I will not kill you at this time. You carried the ark of the Lord GOD before my father David. And you shared in all that my father suffered."
27 So Solomon sent Abiathar away from being a priest of the LORD. This fulfilled the word the LORD had spoken at Shiloh against the house of Eli.
28 When the news reached Joab, he fled to the tent of the LORD. He had joined with Adonijah but not with Absalom. He took hold of the horns of the altar.
29 Someone told King Solomon, "Joab has fled to the tent of the LORD. He is now beside the altar." So Solomon sent Benaiah son of Jehoiada and said, "Go, strike him down!"

30 Benaiah entered the tent of the LORD. He said to Joab, "The king says, 'Come out!'" But Joab replied, "No, I will die here." So Benaiah brought the message back to the king. He said, "This is how Joab answered me."
31 The king replied, "Do as he says. Strike him down and bury him. So remove the innocent blood that Joab shed from me and from my father's house.

32 The LORD will bring his bloodshed back on his own head. Without my father David's knowing, he struck down two men better and more right than he was. He killed Abner son of Ner, leader of Israel's army, and Amasa son of Jether, leader of Judah's army.
33 Their blood will come back on the heads of Joab and his sons forever. But for David, his sons, his house, and his throne, there will be peace from the LORD forever."
34 So Benaiah son of Jehoiada went up and struck Joab down. He killed him. Joab was buried at his own home in the wilderness.
35 The king put Benaiah son of Jehoiada over the army in Joab's place. And he put Zadok the priest in Abiathar's place.
36 Then the king called for Shimei and said to him, "Build a house for yourself in Jerusalem and live there. Do not go anywhere else.
37 On the day you go out and cross the Kidron Valley, you will surely die. Your blood will be on your own head."
38 "What you said is fair," Shimei replied. "Your servant will do as my lord the king has said." So Shimei lived in Jerusalem for a long time.
39 But after three years, two of Shimei's slaves ran away to Achish son of Maacah, king of Gath. Someone told Shimei, "Look, your slaves are in Gath."
40 So Shimei put a saddle on his donkey and set out to Achish at Gath to look for his slaves. He brought them back from Gath.
41 When Solomon was told that Shimei had gone from Jerusalem to Gath and had returned,

42 the king called for Shimei and said to him, "Did I not make you promise by the LORD? Did I not warn you, 'On the day you leave and go elsewhere, you will surely die'? And you told me, 'What you said is fair; I will obey.'
43 So why have you not kept your promise to the LORD and the command I gave you?"
44 The king also said, "You know in your heart all the evil you did to my father David. So the LORD will bring your evil back on your own head.
45 But King Solomon will be blessed. And David's throne will stay secure before the LORD forever."
46 Then the king ordered Benaiah son of Jehoiada. He went out and struck Shimei down, and he died. So the kingdom became strong and secure in Solomon's hand.

title: 1 Kings 3
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — 1 Kings
paragraphs: 1 Later, Solomon married Pharaoh's daughter to make peace with Egypt. He brought her to the City of David. He was still building his palace, the house of the LORD, and the wall around Jerusalem.
2 The people were still offering sacrifices at the high places. A house for the Name of the LORD had not yet been built.
3 Solomon loved the LORD and followed the rules of his father David. But he still offered sacrifices and burned incense at the high places.
4 The king went to Gibeon to offer a sacrifice. It was the most important high place. Solomon offered a thousand burnt offerings on the altar there.
5 One night at Gibeon the LORD appeared to Solomon in a dream. God said, "Ask, and I will give it to you!"
6 Solomon replied, "You have shown great love to your servant, my father David. He walked before you with a faithful, right, and honest heart.

7 You kept showing this great love. You gave him a son to sit on his throne today. Now, O LORD my God, you have made your servant king in my father David's place. But I am only a little child. I do not know how to lead.
8 Your servant is here among the people you have chosen. They are too many to count.
9 So **give your servant a discerning heart** to judge your people. Help me tell the difference between good and evil. Who is able to lead this great people of yours?"
10 The Lord was pleased that Solomon had asked for this.
11 So God said to him, "You did not ask for a long life or wealth for yourself. You did not ask for the death of your enemies. You asked for wisdom to judge fairly.
12 Look, I will do what you have asked. I will give you a wise and discerning heart. No one before you was like this, and no one after you will be.
13 I will also give you what you did not ask for. I will give you wealth and honor. No other king will be like you in all your days.
14 If you walk in my ways and keep my rules and commands, as your father David did, I will give you a long life."

15 Then Solomon woke up. He saw it had been a dream. He went back to Jerusalem and stood before the ark of the covenant of the Lord. He offered burnt offerings and peace offerings. Then he gave a big meal for all his servants.
16 At that time two prostitutes came to the king and stood before him.
17 One woman said, "Please, my lord, this woman and I live in the same house. I had a baby while she was in the house.
18 Three days later, this woman also had a baby. We were alone. No one else was in the house with us.
19 During the night this woman's son died. She rolled on top of him in her sleep.
20 So she got up in the middle of the night. She took my son from my side while I was asleep. She laid him in her arms and put her dead son next to me.
21 The next morning, I got up to feed my baby. I found he was dead. But when I looked closely, I saw he was not the son I had borne."
22 "No," said the other woman, "the living one is my son. The dead one is your son." But the first woman said, "No, the dead one is yours. The living one is mine." So they argued before the king.
23 Then the king said, "This woman says, 'My son is alive and yours is dead.' That woman says, 'No, your son is dead and mine is alive.'"
24 The king said, "Bring me a sword." So they brought him a sword.
25 The king said, "**Now divide the living child in two.** Give half to one woman and half to the other."

26 Then the woman whose son was alive spoke to the king. She felt deep love and pity for her son. "Please, my lord," she said, "give her the living baby. Do not kill him!" But the other woman said, "He will be neither mine nor yours. Cut him in two!"
27 Then the king gave his decision: "Give the living baby to the first woman. Do not kill him. She is his mother."
28 All Israel heard of the king's judgment. They were filled with deep respect for him. They saw that the wisdom of God was in him to judge fairly.

title: 1 Kings 4
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — 1 Kings
paragraphs: 1 So King Solomon ruled over all of Israel.
2 These were his top leaders: Azariah son of Zadok was the priest.
3 Elihoreph and Ahijah, the sons of Shisha, were writers for the king. Jehoshaphat son of Ahilud was the record keeper.
4 Benaiah son of Jehoiada was the leader of the army. Zadok and Abiathar were priests.
5 Azariah son of Nathan was the leader of the governors. Zabud son of Nathan was a priest and the king's advisor.
6 Ahishar was in charge of the palace. Adoniram son of Abda was in charge of the forced work groups.
7 Solomon had twelve governors over all Israel. They supplied food for the king and his royal house. Each one supplied food for one month of the year.
8 These were their names: Ben-hur, in the hill country of Ephraim.
9 Ben-deker, in Makaz, Shaalbim, Beth-shemesh, and Elon-beth-hanan.
10 Ben-hesed, in Arubboth. Socoh and all the land of Hepher belonged to him.
11 Ben-abinadab, in Naphath-dor. Taphath, a daughter of Solomon, was his wife.
12 Baana son of Ahilud, in Taanach, Megiddo, and all of Beth-shean. This was next to Zarethan below Jezreel. His area went from Beth-shean to Abel-meholah, past Jokmeam.

13 Ben-geber, in Ramoth-gilead. The villages of Jair son of Manasseh in Gilead belonged to him. He also had the region of Argob in Bashan. That region had sixty large cities with strong walls and bronze gate bars.
14 Ahinadab son of Iddo, in Mahanaim.
15 Ahimaaz, in Naphtali. He had married Basemath, a daughter of Solomon.
16 Baana son of Hushai, in Asher and Aloth.
17 Jehoshaphat son of Paruah, in Issachar.
18 Shimei son of Ela, in Benjamin.
19 Geber son of Uri, in the land of Gilead. This included the lands of Sihon king of the Amorites and Og king of Bashan. There was also one governor in the land of Judah.
20 The people of Judah and Israel were as many as sand by the sea. They ate and drank and were full of joy.

21 Solomon ruled over all the kingdoms from the Euphrates River to the land of the Philistines. His rule reached as far as the border of Egypt. These kingdoms brought gifts and served Solomon all his life.
22 Solomon's daily food supplies were thirty cors of fine flour and sixty cors of ground grain.
23 He also needed ten well-fed oxen, twenty oxen from the fields, and a hundred sheep. He needed deer, gazelles, wild goats, and fat birds.
24 Solomon ruled over everything west of the Euphrates River. He ruled all the kingdoms from Tiphsah to Gaza. He had peace on every side.
25 All through Solomon's days, Judah and Israel lived safely. From Dan to Beersheba, each person sat safely under his own grape vine and fig tree.
26 Solomon had 4,000 horse stalls for his chariot horses. He also had 12,000 horses.
27 Each month one of the governors supplied food for King Solomon. They also fed all who came to his table. They made sure nothing was missing.
28 They also brought their share of barley grain and dry grass. This was for the chariot horses and other horses.
29 God gave Solomon wisdom and very deep understanding. He gave him wisdom too big to measure, like the sand by the sea.
30 Solomon's wisdom was greater than the wisdom of all the men of the East. It was greater than all the wisdom of Egypt.
31 He was more wise than any other man. He was wiser than Ethan the Ezrahite. He was wiser than Heman, Calcol, and Darda, the sons of Mahol. His name became famous in all the nations around him.
32 Solomon wrote three thousand wise sayings. His songs numbered one thousand and five.
33 He spoke about trees, from the cedar in Lebanon to the hyssop that grows on the wall. He also taught about animals, birds, animals that crawl, and fish.
34 People from all nations came to listen to Solomon's wisdom. The kings of the earth heard of his wisdom and sent them.

title: 1 Kings 5
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — 1 Kings
paragraphs: 1 Hiram king of Tyre heard that Solomon was chosen king in his father's place. So Hiram sent messengers to Solomon. Hiram had always been a friend of David.
2 Then Solomon sent word to Hiram:
3 "You know well that my father David had wars on every side. So he could not build a house for the Name of the LORD his God. He had to wait until the LORD defeated his enemies.
4 But now the LORD my God has given me rest on every side. I have no enemy and no trouble.
5 Look, I plan to build a house for the Name of the LORD my God. The LORD said to my father David, 'I will put your son on your throne in your place. He will build the house for My Name.'

6 So please order that cedar trees from Lebanon be cut down for me. My servants will work with your servants. I will pay your servants the pay you decide. You know that no one among us cuts trees as well as the Sidonians."
7 When Hiram heard Solomon's message, he was very glad. He said, "Praise the LORD this day! He has given David a wise son over this great people!"
8 Then Hiram sent a reply to Solomon, saying: "I have received your message. I will do all you want with the cedar and cypress wood.

9 My servants will bring the logs from Lebanon to the Sea. I will float them as rafts by sea to the place you choose. There I will take the logs apart, and you can carry them away. In return, you can meet my needs by giving my house food."
10 So Hiram gave Solomon all the cedar and cypress wood he wanted.
11 Each year Solomon gave Hiram 20,000 cors of wheat as food for his house. He also gave 20,000 baths of pure olive oil.
12 The LORD gave Solomon wisdom, as He had promised him. There was peace between Hiram and Solomon. The two of them made an agreement.
13 Then King Solomon called up a work force of 30,000 men from all Israel.
14 He sent them to Lebanon in monthly turns of 10,000 men. They would spend one month in Lebanon and two months at home. Adoniram was in charge of the work force.
15 Solomon had 70,000 carriers and 80,000 stone workers in the mountains.
16 He also had 3,300 supervisors who watched the workers.
17 The king told them to cut out large, costly stones. These stones would lay the base of the temple with shaped stones.
18 So Solomon's builders and Hiram's builders worked together. The Gebalites helped them. They cut the stone and prepared the wood and stone for the temple.

title: 1 Kings 6
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — 1 Kings
paragraphs: 1 Solomon began to build the house of the LORD. This was 480 years after the people of Israel left Egypt. It was the month of Ziv, the second month. It was the fourth year of Solomon's rule over Israel.
2 The house that King Solomon built for the LORD was sixty cubits long. It was twenty cubits wide and thirty cubits high.
3 The porch in front of the main hall was twenty cubits long. It went across the full width of the temple. It stuck out ten cubits in front of the temple.
4 He also made narrow windows high in the temple walls.
5 Solomon built side rooms against the walls of the temple. They went all around the temple and the inner sanctuary.

6 The bottom floor was five cubits wide. The middle floor was six cubits wide. The third floor was seven cubits wide. He made wall ledges outside the temple. So the beams did not enter the temple walls.
7 Workers cut the stones at the quarry first. So no hammer, chisel, or iron tool was heard at the temple.
8 The door to the bottom floor was on the south side. A stairway led up to the middle floor. From there it led to the third floor.
9 So Solomon built the temple and finished it. He put a roof of cedar beams and cedar planks on top.
10 He built side rooms all along the temple. Each one was five cubits high. He joined them to the temple with cedar beams.
11 Then the word of the LORD came to Solomon. He said:
12 "You are building this temple. If you follow my rules, obey my laws, and keep all my commands, I will do what I promised your father David through you.
13 I will live among the people of Israel. I will not leave my people Israel."
14 So Solomon built the temple and finished it.
15 He covered the inside walls with cedar boards from floor to ceiling. He covered the floor with cypress boards.
16 He used cedar boards to make a wall at the back. This wall was twenty cubits from the back of the temple. It went from floor to ceiling. This made the inner sanctuary, the Most Holy Place.
17 The main hall in front of this room was forty cubits long.
18 The cedar inside the temple was carved with gourds and open flowers. Everything was cedar. You could not see any stone.
19 Solomon prepared the inner sanctuary inside the temple. He would put the ark of the covenant of the LORD there.
20 The inner sanctuary was twenty cubits long. It was twenty cubits wide and twenty cubits high. He covered the inside with pure gold. He also covered the altar with cedar.
21 Solomon covered the inside of the temple with pure gold. He hung gold chains across the front of the inner sanctuary. He covered it with gold.
22 So he covered the whole inside of the temple with gold. He did this until everything was finished. He also covered the altar of the inner sanctuary with gold.
23 In the inner sanctuary he made two cherubim from olive wood. Each one was ten cubits high.
24 One wing of the first cherub was five cubits long. The other wing was five cubits long too. So the full wingspan was ten cubits.
25 The second cherub was also ten cubits. Both cherubim had the same size and shape.
26 The height of each cherub was ten cubits.

27 He placed the cherubim inside the innermost room of the temple. Their wings were spread out. One wing of the first cherub touched one wall. One wing of the second cherub touched the other wall. In the middle of the room, their wingtips touched.
28 He also covered the cherubim with gold.
29 Then he carved the walls all around the temple. He carved cherubim, palm trees, and open flowers. He did this in both the inner and outer rooms.
30 He also covered the temple floor with gold. He did this in both the inner and outer rooms.
31 For the door of the inner sanctuary, Solomon made doors of olive wood. The doorposts had five sides.
32 The double doors were made of olive wood. He carved cherubim, palm trees, and open flowers into them. He covered the cherubim and palm trees with hammered gold.
33 In the same way, he made four-sided doorposts of olive wood for the temple entrance.
34 The two doors were made of cypress wood. Each door had two folding panels.
35 He carved cherubim, palm trees, and open flowers into them. He covered them with gold, hammered evenly over the carvings.
36 Solomon built the inner courtyard. He used three rows of shaped stone and one row of cut cedar beams.
37 The foundation of the house of the LORD was laid in the fourth year of Solomon's rule. It was in the month of Ziv.
38 In his eleventh year, in the eighth month, the month of Bul, the temple was finished. Every part was built exactly as planned. So he built the temple in seven years.

title: 1 Kings 7
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — 1 Kings
paragraphs: 1 Solomon, however, took thirteen years to finish all his palace.
2 He built the House of the Forest of Lebanon. It was a hundred cubits long, fifty cubits wide, and thirty cubits high. It had four rows of cedar pillars holding up the cedar beams.
3 The house was covered with cedar above the beams. The beams rested on the pillars — forty-five beams, fifteen per row.
4 There were three rows of high windows facing one another in three levels.
5 All the doorways had square frames. Their openings faced one another in three levels.
6 Solomon made his hall of pillars fifty cubits long and thirty cubits wide. It had a porch in front. A roof cover with pillars stood in front of the porch.
7 He also built a hall for the throne, the Hall of Justice. He would judge there. It was lined with cedar from floor to ceiling.
8 The palace where Solomon would live stood further back. It was built the same way. He also made a palace like this hall for Pharaoh's daughter, his wife.
9 All these buildings used costly stones. The stones were cut to size and cut with saws inside and out. This was done from the base to the roof edge, and from the outside to the great yard.
10 The base was laid with large, costly stones. Some were ten cubits long, and some were eight cubits long.
11 Above these were fine stones, cut to size, and cedar beams.
12 The great yard had three rows of shaped stone around it. It also had a row of cut cedar beams. The inner yard and porch of the house of the LORD were the same.
13 Now King Solomon sent for Huram from Tyre.

14 Huram's mother was a widow from the tribe of Naphtali. His father was a man of Tyre, a bronze worker. Huram had great skill, understanding, and knowledge in every kind of bronze work. So he came to King Solomon and did all his work.
15 He shaped two bronze pillars. Each one was eighteen cubits high and twelve cubits around.
16 He also made two bronze tops to set on the pillars. Each top was five cubits high.
17 For the tops of the pillars he made a network of woven work, with chain patterns — seven for each top.
18 He also made the pillars with two rows of pomegranates around each woven cover. They covered each top.
19 The tops of the pillars in the porch were shaped like lilies, four cubits high.
20 On the tops of both pillars, just above the round part next to the network, were two hundred pomegranates. They went in rows around each top.
21 So he set up the pillars at the porch of the temple. He named the south pillar Jachin. He named the north pillar Boaz.
22 The tops of the pillars were shaped like lilies. So the work of the pillars was finished.
23 He also made the Sea of cast metal. It was round, ten cubits from edge to edge, five cubits high, and thirty cubits around.
24 Below the edge, round buds went all around it. There were ten per cubit, in two rows, shaped as part of the Sea.
25 The Sea stood on twelve oxen. Three faced north, three west, three south, and three east. The Sea rested on them, with all their back parts toward the center.
26 It was a handbreadth thick. Its edge was shaped like the rim of a cup, like a lily blossom. It could hold two thousand baths.
27 He also made ten rolling stands of bronze. Each was four cubits long, four cubits wide, and three cubits high.
28 The stands were made this way. They had side panels joined to upright posts.
29 On the panels between the posts were lions, oxen, and cherubim. Above the posts was a base on top. Below the lions and oxen were chain patterns of slanted work.
30 Each stand had four bronze wheels with bronze axles. A basin rested on four supports, with chain patterns on each side.

31 The opening at the top of each stand sat inside a crown, one cubit deep. The opening was round like a base, a cubit and a half wide. Carvings circled the opening. But the panels of the stands were square, not round.
32 There were four wheels under the panels. The axles joined the wheels to the stand. Each wheel was a cubit and a half across.
33 The wheels were made like chariot wheels. Their axles, rims, spokes, and hubs were all of cast metal.
34 Each stand had four handles, one at each corner, coming out from the stand.
35 At the top of each stand was a round band half a cubit high. The supports and panels were shaped as one piece with the top.
36 He carved cherubim, lions, and palm trees on the supports and panels, wherever there was space. Chain patterns went all around them.
37 In this way he made the ten stands. They all had the same shape, size, and design.
38 He also made ten bronze basins. Each held forty baths and was four cubits across. There was one basin for each of the ten stands.
39 He set five stands on the south side of the temple and five on the north. He put the Sea on the south side, at the southeast corner of the temple.
40 Huram also made the pots, shovels, and sprinkling bowls. So Huram finished all the work he had done for King Solomon in the house of the LORD:
41 the two pillars; the two bowl-shaped tops on the pillars; the two networks that covered both bowls on the tops of the pillars;
42 the four hundred pomegranates for the two networks (two rows of pomegranates for each network covering the bowl-shaped tops on the pillars);
43 the ten stands; the ten basins on the stands;
44 the Sea; the twelve oxen under the Sea;
45 and the pots, shovels, and sprinkling bowls. Huram made all these items for King Solomon in the house of the LORD from polished bronze.
46 The king had them shaped in clay molds in the plain of the Jordan. This was between Succoth and Zarethan.
47 Solomon left all these items unweighed. There were so many that no one could find the weight of the bronze.
48 Solomon also made all the items for the house of the LORD: the golden altar; the golden table that held the Bread of the Presence;
49 the lampstands of pure gold in front of the inner room, five on the right and five on the left; the gold flowers, lamps, and tongs;
50 the pure gold basins, wick cutters, sprinkling bowls, ladles, and censers; and the gold hinges for the doors of the inner temple (the Most Holy Place) and for the doors of the main hall.

51 So all the work King Solomon did for the house of the LORD was finished. Then Solomon brought in the items his father David had given to God — the silver, the gold, and the items. He placed them in the storerooms of the house of the LORD.

title: 1 Kings 8
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — 1 Kings
paragraphs: 1 At that time Solomon gathered the leaders of Israel in Jerusalem. He called the tribal heads and family leaders. They came to bring up the ark of the covenant of the LORD from Zion, the City of David.
2 All the men of Israel came to King Solomon at the feast. This was in the seventh month, the month of Ethanim.
3 When all the leaders of Israel arrived, the priests took up the ark.
4 They brought up the ark of the LORD and the Tent of Meeting. They also brought all its holy things. The priests and Levites carried them up.
5 King Solomon and all the people of Israel stood before the ark. They offered so many sheep and oxen that no one could count them.
6 Then the priests brought the ark of the covenant of the LORD to its place. They placed it in the inner room of the temple, the Most Holy Place. It rested under the wings of the cherubim.
7 For the cherubim spread their wings over the place of the ark. They covered the ark and its poles.
8 The poles were long enough to be seen from the Holy Place. But people could not see them from outside. They are there to this day.
9 Only the two stone tablets were in the ark. Moses had placed them in it at Horeb. There the LORD made a covenant with Israel after they left Egypt.
10 When the priests came out of the Holy Place, **the cloud filled the temple of the LORD**.
11 The priests could not stand and serve because of the cloud. For the glory of the LORD filled the temple of the LORD.
12 Then Solomon said, "**The LORD has said that he would dwell in a dark cloud**.
13 I have built you a great house. It is a place for you to live in forever."
14 All the people of Israel were standing there. The king turned around and blessed them all.
15 He said, "Blessed be the LORD, the God of Israel. He has done with his own hand what he spoke to my father David. He said,

16 'Since the day I brought my people Israel out of Egypt, I have not chosen any city. I did not choose a city from any tribe to build a house for my Name. But I have chosen David to lead my people Israel.'
17 My father David wanted to build a house for the Name of the LORD, the God of Israel.
18 But the LORD said to my father David, 'It was good that you wanted to build a house for my Name. You did well to have this in your heart.
19 But you are not the one to build it. Your own son will build the house for my Name.'
20 Now the LORD has done what he promised. I have followed my father David. I sit on the throne of Israel, as the LORD said. I have built the house for the Name of the LORD, the God of Israel.
21 I have made a place there for the ark. The ark holds the covenant of the LORD. He made it with our fathers when he brought them out of Egypt."
22 Then Solomon stood before the altar of the LORD. He stood in front of all the people of Israel. He spread out his hands toward heaven.

23 He said, "O LORD, God of Israel, there is no God like you. There is none in heaven above or on earth below. You keep your covenant of faithful love with your servants. They walk before you with all their hearts.
24 You have kept your promise to your servant, my father David. What you said with your mouth, you have done with your hand today.
25 Now, O LORD, God of Israel, keep your promise to my father David. You said, 'You will always have a man to sit on the throne of Israel. But your sons must walk before me, as you have done.'
26 And now, O God of Israel, please do what you promised to your servant, my father David.
27 But will God really live on the earth? **Heaven, even the highest heaven, cannot hold you. How much less this temple I have built**!
28 Yet hear the prayer of your servant, O LORD my God. Hear the cry and the prayer that your servant prays before you today.
29 May your eyes be open toward this temple night and day. You said, 'My Name will be there.' Hear the prayer that your servant prays toward this place.
30 Hear the prayer of your servant and your people Israel. They will pray toward this place. Hear from heaven, your home. Hear and forgive.
31 When a man sins against his neighbor, he must take an oath. When he comes to take an oath before your altar in this temple,
32 then hear from heaven and act. Judge your servants. Punish the guilty man for what he has done. Reward the righteous man for his right living.
33 When your people Israel lose a battle because they sinned against you, they will turn back to you. They will confess your name and pray to you in this temple.
34 Then hear from heaven and forgive the sin of your people Israel. Bring them back to the land you gave to their fathers.
35 When the sky is shut and there is no rain because your people sinned, they will pray toward this place. They will confess your name and turn from their sins, because you punished them.
36 Then hear from heaven and forgive the sin of your servants, your people Israel. Teach them the good way to walk. Send rain on the land you gave to your people.
37 When hunger or sickness comes on the land, or plant disease or mold or locusts or grasshoppers, or when an enemy surrounds their cities, or any sickness comes—
38 then hear the prayer of your people Israel. Each one will know his own troubles. Each one will spread out his hands toward this temple.
39 Hear from heaven, your home. Forgive and act. Repay each man according to all his ways. You know his heart, for you alone know all human hearts.
40 Then they will fear you all the days they live in the land. You gave this land to our fathers.
41 And what about the foreigner who is not from your people Israel? He may come from a faraway land because of your name.
42 People will hear of your great name and mighty hand and strong arm. When the foreigner comes and prays toward this temple,

43 then hear from heaven, your home. Do all that the foreigner asks of you. Then all peoples on earth will know your name. They will fear you, just like your people Israel. They will know that this house I built is called by your Name.
44 Your people may go to war against their enemies, wherever you send them. They may pray to the LORD toward the city you have chosen, and toward the house I have built for your Name.
45 Then hear their prayer from heaven. Help them in their cause.
46 When they sin against you—for there is no one who does not sin—you may become angry with them. You may give them up to an enemy who takes them away to a faraway land.
47 They may come to their senses in that land. They may turn back and pray to you in the land of their enemies. They will say, 'We have sinned and done wrong. We have done evil.'

48 They may turn back to you with all their heart and soul in the land of their enemies. They will pray toward the land you gave their fathers, the city you chose, and the house I built for your Name.
49 Then hear from heaven, your home, their prayer. Help them in their cause.
50 Forgive your people who sinned against you. Forgive all the wrong things they did against you. Help their enemies show them mercy.
51 For they are your people and your special people. You brought them out of Egypt, out of the hot furnace for melting iron.
52 May your eyes be open to the prayer of your servant and of your people Israel. Listen to them whenever they call to you.
53 For you, O Lord GOD, set them apart from all peoples of the earth. They are your special people. You spoke this through your servant Moses when you brought our fathers out of Egypt."
54 Solomon finished this whole prayer to the LORD. He had been kneeling before the altar of the LORD. His hands were spread out toward heaven. Then he stood up.
55 He stood and blessed all the people of Israel in a loud voice. He said,
56 "Blessed be the LORD! He has given rest to his people Israel, just as he promised. Not one word has failed of all the good promises he made through his servant Moses.
57 May the LORD our God be with us, as he was with our fathers. May he never leave us or turn away from us.
58 May he turn our hearts to himself. May we walk in all his ways. May we keep the commands and rules and laws he gave our fathers.
59 May my prayers to the LORD be near to the LORD our God day and night. Then he will help his servant and his people Israel as each day requires.
60 Then all peoples on earth will know that the LORD is God. There is no other!
61 So give your hearts fully to the LORD our God, as you do today. Walk in his rules and keep his commands."
62 Then the king and all Israel offered sacrifices before the LORD.
63 Solomon offered as peace offerings to the LORD 22,000 oxen and 120,000 sheep. So the king and all the Israelites dedicated the house of the LORD.

64 That same day the king set apart the middle of the courtyard for God. It was in front of the house of the LORD. There he offered the burnt offerings, the grain offerings, and the fat of the peace offerings. The bronze altar before the LORD was too small to hold all these offerings.

65 So Solomon and all Israel kept the feast at that time. A great crowd of people came from Lebo-hamath to the Brook of Egypt. They kept the feast before the LORD our God for seven days and seven more days—fourteen days in all.

66 On the fifteenth day, Solomon sent the people away. They blessed the king and went home. They were joyful and glad in heart. The LORD had done good things for his servant David and for his people Israel.

title: 1 Kings 9
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — 1 Kings
paragraphs: 1 Solomon finished building the temple of the LORD and the king's palace. He did everything he wanted to do.
2 Then the LORD appeared to him a second time. He had appeared to him before at Gibeon.
3 The LORD said to him, "I have heard your prayer and your request. I have set this temple apart as holy. I have placed My Name here forever. My eyes and My heart will be here for all time.
4 "As for you, walk before Me as your father David walked. Have an honest and right heart. Do all that I have commanded you. Keep My rules and laws.
5 "Then I will make your throne over Israel strong forever. I promised your father David this. I said, 'A man from your family will always sit on the throne of Israel.'
6 "But if you or your sons stop following Me, things will be different. If you do not keep the commands and rules I have given you, and if you go to serve other gods and worship them—

7 "then I will take Israel away from the land I have given them. I will send away this temple I have set apart for My Name. Then Israel will be a thing that people mock and laugh at among all nations.
8 "This temple will become a pile of broken stones. All who pass by will be shocked. They will whistle in shock and say, 'Why has the LORD done this to this land and to this temple?'

9 "Others will answer, 'They left the LORD their God. He brought their fathers out of Egypt. But they held on to other gods. They worshiped and served them. So the LORD has brought all this trouble on them.'"
10 Solomon built these two houses over twenty years. He built the temple of the LORD and the king's palace.
11 King Solomon gave twenty towns in Galilee to Hiram king of Tyre. Hiram had given him cedar and cypress wood and gold for everything he wanted.
12 Hiram went out from Tyre to see the towns that Solomon had given him. But he was not pleased with them.
13 Hiram asked, "What are these towns you have given me, my brother?" He called them the Land of Cabul. People still call them by that name today.
14 Hiram had sent the king 120 talents of gold.

15 This is the account of the forced workers that King Solomon made work. They built the temple of the LORD, his own palace, the supporting walls, and the wall of Jerusalem. They also built Hazor, Megiddo, and Gezer.
16 Pharaoh king of Egypt had attacked Gezer and taken it. He had set it on fire and killed the Canaanites in the city. Then he gave it as a wedding gift to his daughter, Solomon's wife.
17 So Solomon rebuilt Gezer and Lower Beth-horon.
18 He also rebuilt Baalath and Tamar in the Wilderness of Judah.
19 He built all the storage cities that he had for his chariots and horses. He built whatever he wanted in Jerusalem, Lebanon, and all his land.
20 Some people remained from the Amorites, Hittites, Perizzites, Hivites, and Jebusites. These people were not Israelites.
21 Their children still lived in the land. The Israelites had not been able to fully destroy them. Solomon made these people work as forced workers. They still do this work today.
22 But Solomon did not make any Israelites slaves. They were his soldiers, his servants, his officers, his captains, and the commanders of his chariots and horsemen.
23 They were also the chief leaders over Solomon's work. There were 550 supervisors over the people who did the work.
24 Pharaoh's daughter came up from the City of David to the palace Solomon had built for her. After that, he built the supporting walls.
25 Three times a year Solomon offered burnt offerings and peace offerings. He offered them on the altar he had built for the LORD. He also burned incense before the LORD. So he finished the temple.
26 King Solomon also gathered a group of ships at Ezion-geber. This place is near Eloth in Edom, on the shore of the Red Sea.
27 Hiram sent his servants to serve with Solomon's servants. These were men who knew the sea.
28 They sailed to Ophir and brought back gold from there. They brought 420 talents of gold to Solomon.

title: 1 Kings 10
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — 1 Kings
paragraphs: 1 The queen of Sheba heard the news about Solomon and the LORD's name. She came to test him with hard questions.
2 She came to Jerusalem with a big group of travelers. Her camels carried spices, much gold, and costly stones. She came to Solomon and told him everything on her mind.
3 Solomon answered all her questions. Nothing was too hard for the king to explain.
4 The queen of Sheba saw all of Solomon's wisdom. She saw the palace he had built.
5 She saw the food at his table and the seats of his servants. She saw the clothing of his servants and wine servants. She saw the offerings he gave at the house of the LORD. She was amazed.
6 She said to the king, "The report in my country about your words and wisdom is true.
7 But I did not believe it until I came and saw with my own eyes. Not even half was told to me. Your wisdom and wealth are far greater than the report.
8 How blessed are your men! How blessed are your servants who stand before you and hear your wisdom!
9 Praise the LORD your God! He was pleased with you and made you king of Israel. Because the LORD loves Israel forever, He made you king. You will bring justice and what is right."
10 Then she gave the king 120 talents of gold, many spices, and costly stones. Never again did anyone bring so many spices as the queen of Sheba gave to King Solomon.
11 Hiram's ships also brought gold from Ophir. They brought a large load of almug wood and costly stones from Ophir.
12 The king made the almug wood into steps for the house of the LORD and for the palace. He also made harps for the singers. Never before had such almug wood come, and none has been seen since.
13 King Solomon gave the queen of Sheba all she wanted. He gave whatever she asked, besides his own royal gifts. Then she went back to her own country with her servants.
14 Each year, 666 talents of gold came to Solomon.
15 This did not count the money from merchants, traders, all the Arabian kings, and the governors of the land.
16 King Solomon made two hundred large shields of beaten gold. Each shield used six hundred shekels of gold.
17 He also made three hundred small shields of beaten gold. Each shield used three minas of gold. The king put them in the House of the Forest of Lebanon.
18 The king also made a great throne of ivory. He covered it with pure gold.
19 The throne had six steps. Its back had a rounded top. There were armrests on both sides of the seat. A lion stood beside each armrest.
20 Twelve lions stood on the six steps, one at each end of each step. Nothing like this was ever made for any kingdom.
21 All of King Solomon's cups were gold. All the dishes in the House of the Forest of Lebanon were pure gold. There was no silver, because silver was not valued in Solomon's days.
22 The king had ships of Tarshish at sea with Hiram's ships. Once every three years, the ships of Tarshish came back. They brought gold, silver, ivory, apes, and peacocks.
23 So King Solomon was greater than all the kings of the earth in riches and wisdom.
24 The whole world wanted to meet Solomon. They came to hear the wisdom God had put in his heart.
25 Year after year, each visitor brought a gift. They brought silver and gold things, clothing, weapons, spices, horses, and mules.
26 Solomon gathered 1,400 chariots and 12,000 horses. He placed them in the chariot cities and also with him in Jerusalem.
27 The king made silver as common as stones in Jerusalem. He made cedar as common as sycamore trees in the low hills.
28 Solomon's horses came from Egypt and Kue. The king's merchants bought them from Kue.
29 A chariot from Egypt cost six hundred shekels of silver. A horse cost a hundred and fifty. They also sold them to all the kings of the Hittites and the kings of Aram.

title: 1 Kings 11
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — 1 Kings
paragraphs: 1 King Solomon loved many women from other lands. He loved the daughter of Pharaoh. He also loved women of Moab, Ammon, Edom, Sidon, and the Hittites.
2 These women were from nations the LORD had warned Israel about. He said, "Do not marry their people. They will turn your hearts to their gods." But Solomon still loved them.
3 He had seven hundred wives from royal families. He also had three hundred concubines. His wives turned his heart away.
4 When Solomon grew old, his wives turned his heart to other gods. He did not fully follow the LORD his God, as his father David had.
5 Solomon followed Ashtoreth, the goddess of the Sidonians. He also followed Milcom, the hated idol of the Ammonites.
6 So Solomon did evil in the sight of the LORD. Unlike his father David, he did not fully follow the LORD.
7 On a hill east of Jerusalem, Solomon built a high place. It was for Chemosh, the hated idol of Moab. He also built one for Molech, the hated idol of the Ammonites.
8 He did the same for all his foreign wives. They burned sweet smoke and offered gifts to their gods.
9 The LORD grew angry with Solomon. His heart had turned away from the LORD, the God of Israel. The LORD had appeared to him two times.
10 The LORD had told him clearly not to follow other gods. But Solomon did not keep the LORD's command.
11 Then the LORD said to Solomon, "You have done this. You have not kept My covenant and My laws. So I will take the kingdom from you. I will give it to your servant.
12 But for the sake of your father David, I will not do it while you live. I will take it from your son's hand.
13 Yet I will not take the whole kingdom from him. I will give one tribe to your son. I do this for My servant David and for Jerusalem, the city I have chosen."
14 Then the LORD raised up an enemy against Solomon. His name was Hadad the Edomite, from the royal family of Edom.
15 Earlier, when David was in Edom, Joab the leader of the army went to bury the dead. He killed every male in Edom.
16 Joab and all Israel stayed there six months. He killed every male in Edom.
17 But Hadad was still a young boy. He ran away to Egypt with some Edomites who served his father.
18 They left Midian and went to Paran. They took men from Paran with them. They went to Pharaoh king of Egypt. Pharaoh gave Hadad a house, land, and food.
19 Hadad pleased Pharaoh very much. Pharaoh gave him the sister of Queen Tahpenes, his own wife, to marry.
20 The sister of Tahpenes had a son for Hadad. They named him Genubath. Tahpenes raised him in Pharaoh's palace. Genubath lived there among Pharaoh's sons.
21 In Egypt, Hadad heard that David had died and joined his fathers. He also heard that Joab, the leader of the army, was dead. Hadad said to Pharaoh, "Let me go. I want to return to my own country."
22 Pharaoh asked him, "What do you lack here with me? Why do you want to go back to your country?" Hadad said, "Nothing. But please let me go."
23 God raised up another enemy against Solomon. His name was Rezon son of Eliada. He had run from his master, Hadadezer king of Zobah.
24 Rezon gathered men around him. When David killed the men of Zobah, Rezon led a group of robbers. They went to Damascus. They settled there and ruled.
25 Rezon was Israel's enemy all the days of Solomon. He added to the trouble caused by Hadad. Rezon ruled over Aram and hated Israel.
26 Jeroboam son of Nebat was from the tribe of Ephraim, from Zeredah. His mother was a widow named Zeruah. Jeroboam served Solomon, but he turned against the king.
27 This is the story of how he turned against the king. Solomon was building the side walls. He was fixing the gap in the wall of the city of his father David.
28 Jeroboam was a strong and brave man. Solomon saw that the young man was a good worker. So he put him in charge of all the workers from the house of Joseph.
29 At that time, the prophet Ahijah the Shilonite met Jeroboam on the road. Jeroboam was going out of Jerusalem. Ahijah had a new cloak on. The two of them were alone in the open field.
30 Ahijah took hold of the new cloak he was wearing. He tore it into twelve pieces.
31 He said to Jeroboam, "Take ten pieces for yourself. This is what the LORD, the God of Israel, says: 'Look, I will take the kingdom from Solomon's hand. I will give you ten tribes.
32 But one tribe will stay with him. I do this for My servant David and for Jerusalem, the city I have chosen out of all the tribes of Israel.

33 They have left Me. They worship Ashtoreth, the goddess of the Sidonians. They worship Chemosh, the god of the Moabites. They worship Milcom, the god of the Ammonites. They have not lived My way. They have not done what is right in My eyes. They have not kept My laws, as Solomon's father David did.
34 But I will not take the whole kingdom from Solomon's hand. I have made him ruler all the days of his life. I do this for David My servant. I chose him because he kept My commands and laws.
35 But I will take ten tribes of the kingdom from his son's hand. I will give them to you.
36 I will give one tribe to his son. So My servant David will always have a lamp before Me in Jerusalem. That is the city I chose to put My Name.
37 But as for you, I will take you. You will rule over all that your heart wants. You will be king over Israel.

38 Listen to all that I command you. Live My way. Do what is right in My sight. Keep My laws and commands, as My servant David did. Then I will be with you. I will build you a strong family line of kings, just as I did for David. I will give Israel to you.
39 Because of this, I will bring David's family low—but not forever.'"
40 So Solomon tried to kill Jeroboam. But Jeroboam ran away to Egypt. He went to Shishak king of Egypt. He stayed there until Solomon died.
41 The rest of the acts of Solomon, all that he did, and his wisdom—are they not written in the Book of the Acts of Solomon?
42 Solomon reigned in Jerusalem over all Israel for forty years.
43 Then Solomon died and joined his fathers. He was buried in the city of his father David. His son Rehoboam became king in his place.

title: 1 Kings 12
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — 1 Kings
paragraphs: 1 Then Rehoboam went to Shechem. All Israel had gone there to make him king.
2 Jeroboam son of Nebat heard about this. He was still in Egypt, where he had run from King Solomon.
3 So they sent for Jeroboam. He and the whole crowd of Israel came to Rehoboam and said,
4 "Your father put a heavy load on us. Now make our work lighter, and we will serve you."
5 Rehoboam answered, "Go away for three days. Then come back to me." So the people went away.
6 Then King Rehoboam asked the older men who had served his father Solomon. "How should I answer these people?" he asked.
7 They replied, "Be a servant to these people today. Serve them and speak kind words to them. Then they will always be your servants."
8 But Rehoboam did not take the older men's advice. Instead, he asked the young men, his own friends, who served him.
9 He asked them, "What should I say to these people? They told me, 'Make the load your father put on us lighter.'"
10 The young men, his friends, replied, "Say this to them: 'My little finger is thicker than my father's body!
11 My father put a heavy load on you. I will make it heavier. My father beat you with whips. I will beat you with sharp whips.'"
12 After three days, Jeroboam and all the people came back to Rehoboam. The king had said, "Come back on the third day."
13 And the king answered the people in a hard way. He did not take the older men's advice.
14 He spoke to them as the young men had said. "My father made your load heavy. I will make it heavier. My father beat you with whips. I will beat you with sharp whips."
15 So the king did not listen to the people. The LORD made this happen, to keep his word to Jeroboam son of Nebat through Ahijah the Shilonite.

16 All Israel saw that the king would not listen to them. So they answered the king: "We have no share with David. We have nothing from the son of Jesse. Go home, Israel! Look after your own house, David!" So the Israelites went home.
17 But Rehoboam still ruled the Israelites who lived in the cities of Judah.
18 Then King Rehoboam sent out Adoram, who was in charge of the hard work crews. But all Israel threw stones at him until he died. King Rehoboam jumped into his chariot quickly and ran to Jerusalem.
19 So to this day Israel has turned away from David's family.
20 All Israel heard that Jeroboam had come back. They called him to the meeting and made him king over all Israel. Only the tribe of Judah followed David's family.
21 When Rehoboam came to Jerusalem, he gathered the whole house of Judah and the tribe of Benjamin. He had 180,000 best soldiers, to fight Israel and win back the kingdom for himself.
22 But God spoke to Shemaiah the man of God:
23 "Tell Rehoboam son of Solomon, king of Judah, all the people of Judah and Benjamin, and the rest of the people,

24 this is what the LORD says: 'Do not go up and fight your brothers, the Israelites. Each of you must go home, for this is from me.'" So they listened to the LORD's word and turned back, as the LORD had said.
25 Then Jeroboam built up Shechem in the hill country of Ephraim and lived there. From there he went out and built Penuel.
26 Jeroboam said in his heart, "Now the kingdom might go back to David's family.
27 If these people go up to offer sacrifices in the house of the LORD at Jerusalem, their hearts will return to their lord, Rehoboam king of Judah. Then they will kill me and go back to Rehoboam."
28 After asking for advice, the king made two golden calves. He said to the people, "Going up to Jerusalem is too much for you. **Here are your gods, Israel, who brought you up out of Egypt**."
29 He set up one calf in Bethel, and the other in Dan.
30 And this thing became a sin. The people walked as far as Dan to worship one of the calves.
31 Jeroboam also built small temples on the hills. He chose priests from all kinds of people, who were not Levites.

32 Jeroboam set up a feast on the fifteenth day of the eighth month, like the feast in Judah. He offered sacrifices on the altar in Bethel, to the calves he had made. He also put priests in Bethel for the small temples on the hills.

33 On the fifteenth day of the eighth month, a month he had chosen himself, Jeroboam offered sacrifices on the altar he had built in Bethel. He set up a feast for the Israelites, offered sacrifices on the altar, and burned sweet-smelling spice.

title: 1 Kings 13
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — 1 Kings
paragraphs: 1 Suddenly, while Jeroboam stood by the altar to burn incense, a man of God came. He came from Judah to Bethel by the word of the LORD.

2 He spoke against the altar by the word of the LORD. He said, "O altar, altar! The LORD says this: 'A son named Josiah will be born to the house of David. On you he will offer the priests of the high places as sacrifice. They burn incense on you. Human bones will burn on you.'"
3 That day the man of God gave a sign. He said, "The LORD has spoken this sign. The altar will split apart. The ashes on it will pour out."
4 King Jeroboam stood at the altar in Bethel. He heard the words of the man of God. He stretched out his hand and said, "Seize him!" But his hand dried up. He could not pull it back.
5 And the altar split apart. The ashes poured out. This matched the sign the man of God had given by the word of the LORD.
6 Then the king said to the man of God, "Pray to the LORD your God for me. Ask Him to heal my hand." So the man of God prayed to the LORD. The king's hand was healed as before.
7 Then the king said to the man of God, "Come home with me. Rest and eat. I will give you a reward."
8 But the man of God replied, "Even if you gave me half of all you own, I would not go with you. I will not eat bread or drink water here.
9 For the word of the LORD gave me this command: 'You must not eat bread or drink water. You must not go back the way you came.'"
10 So the man of God went home by another road. He did not return by the road to Bethel.
11 Now an old prophet lived in Bethel. His sons came and told him everything. They told what the man of God did that day in Bethel. They told what he said to the king.
12 Their father asked, "Which way did he go?" His sons showed him the road. The man of God from Judah had taken that road.
13 So the prophet said to his sons, "Get the donkey ready for me." They got the donkey ready. He got on it.
14 He went after the man of God. He found him sitting under an oak tree. He asked, "Are you the man of God who came from Judah?" "I am," he replied.
15 Then the prophet said, "Come home with me and eat some bread."
16 But the man replied, "I cannot return with you. I cannot eat bread or drink water with you here.
17 For the word of the LORD told me: 'You must not eat bread or drink water there. You must not go back the way you came.'"

18 Then the prophet said, "I am a prophet like you. An angel spoke to me by the word of the LORD. He said, 'Bring him back to your house. Let him eat bread and drink water.'" But the old prophet was lying.
19 So the man of God went back with him. He ate bread in his house and drank water.
20 They were sitting at the table. Then the word of the LORD came to the prophet who had brought him back.
21 The prophet cried out to the man of God from Judah. "The LORD says this: 'You did not obey the word of the LORD. You did not keep the command of the LORD your God.
22 You went back. You ate bread and drank water in the place He told you not to. So your body will not reach your family tomb.'"
23 After the man of God finished eating and drinking, the old prophet got the donkey ready for him.
24 As he went on his way, a lion met him on the road. The lion killed him. His body lay on the road. The donkey and the lion stood beside it.
25 Some men passed by. They saw the body in the road with the lion beside it. They went and reported it in the city where the old prophet lived.

26 The prophet who had brought him back heard this. He said, "It is the man of God who disobeyed the command of the LORD. So the LORD gave him to the lion. The lion has hurt him and killed him. This matches the word the LORD spoke to him."
27 Then the old prophet said to his sons, "Get the donkey ready for me." So they got it ready.
28 He went and found the body in the road. The donkey and the lion stood beside it. The lion had not eaten the body. It had not hurt the donkey.
29 So the old prophet lifted up the body of the man of God. He laid it on the donkey. He brought it back to his own city to mourn and bury him.
30 He laid the body in his own tomb. They cried loudly over him, "Oh, my brother!"
31 After he buried him, the prophet said to his sons, "When I die, bury me in the tomb where the man of God is buried. Lay my bones beside his bones.

32 For he spoke a message by the word of the LORD. He spoke against the altar in Bethel. He spoke against all the worship places on the high places in the cities of Samaria. That message will surely come true."

33 Even after these events, Jeroboam did not turn from his evil ways. Again he appointed priests for the high places. He took them from every class of people. He appointed anyone who wanted to be a priest of the high places.
34 This was the sin of the house of Jeroboam. It led to its end and ruin from the earth.

title: 1 Kings 14
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — 1 Kings
paragraphs: 1 At that time, Abijah son of Jeroboam became sick.
2 Jeroboam said to his wife, "Get up and dress so no one knows you. Go to Shiloh.
3 Ahijah the prophet lives there. He told me I would be king. Take ten loaves of bread, some cakes, and a jar of honey. He will tell you what will happen to the boy."
4 Jeroboam's wife did this. She got up and went to Shiloh. She came to Ahijah's house. Ahijah could no longer see well; he was very old.
5 But the LORD had said to Ahijah, "Look, the wife of Jeroboam is coming. She will ask you about her son, for he is sick. Say this and that to her. She will pretend to be someone else."
6 When Ahijah heard her feet at the door, he said, "Come in, wife of Jeroboam! Why do you pretend? I must give you bad news.
7 Go and tell Jeroboam this. The LORD, the God of Israel, says: 'I lifted you up from the people. I made you ruler over my people Israel.

8 I took the kingdom from the family of David and gave it to you. But you were not like my servant David. He obeyed my commands. He followed me with his whole heart. He did only what was right in my eyes.
9 You have done more evil than all who came before you. You have made other gods and metal idols. You have made me angry. You have thrown me behind your back.

10 Because of this, look — I am bringing trouble on the family of Jeroboam. I will kill every male in his family, both slave and free, in Israel. I will burn up the family of Jeroboam like waste, until it is gone.
11 Dogs will eat anyone of Jeroboam who dies in the city. Birds will eat anyone who dies in the field. The LORD has spoken.'
12 As for you, get up and go home. When your feet enter the city, the child will die.
13 All Israel will cry for him and bury him. He alone in Jeroboam's family will have a proper burial. The LORD, the God of Israel, has found good only in him.
14 The LORD will raise up a king over Israel for himself. That king will cut off the family of Jeroboam. This is the day — yes, even today!

15 The LORD will strike Israel. Israel will shake like a reed in the water. He will pull Israel out of this good land that he gave their fathers. He will scatter them beyond the Euphrates River. They have made Asherah poles and made the LORD angry.
16 He will give Israel up because of the sins of Jeroboam. He sinned and led Israel to sin too."
17 Then Jeroboam's wife got up and went to Tirzah. As soon as she stepped through the door of the house, the boy died.
18 They buried him, and all Israel cried for him. This happened just as the LORD had said through his servant Ahijah the prophet.
19 The rest of the acts of Jeroboam — his wars and his rule — are written in the Book of the Acts of the Kings of Israel.
20 Jeroboam ruled for twenty-two years. Then he died and joined his fathers. His son Nadab ruled in his place.

21 Meanwhile, Rehoboam son of Solomon ruled in Judah. He was forty-one years old when he became king. He ruled seventeen years in Jerusalem. The LORD chose Jerusalem from all the tribes of Israel to put his Name there. His mother's name was Naamah the Ammonite.
22 Judah did evil in the eyes of the LORD. They sinned and made him more jealous and angry than their fathers had.
23 They built for themselves worship places, sacred stones, and Asherah poles. They built them on every high hill and under every green tree.
24 There were even men who sold their bodies at the shrines in the land. They copied the evil ways of the nations that the LORD had driven out before the people of Israel.
25 In the fifth year of Rehoboam's rule, Shishak king of Egypt came up and attacked Jerusalem.
26 He took the treasures of the house of the LORD and the royal palace. He took everything, including all the gold shields that Solomon had made.
27 Then King Rehoboam made bronze shields in their place. He gave them to the care of the captains of the guard at the entrance to the royal palace.
28 Whenever the king entered the house of the LORD, the guards carried the shields. Then they returned them to the guardroom.
29 The rest of the acts of Rehoboam, with all that he did, are written in the Book of the Acts of the Kings of Judah.
30 There was war between Rehoboam and Jeroboam all their days.
31 Rehoboam died and joined his fathers. They buried him with them in the City of David. His mother's name was Naamah the Ammonite. His son Abijam ruled in his place.

title: 1 Kings 15
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — 1 Kings
paragraphs: 1 In year 18 of King Jeroboam son of Nebat, Abijam became king of Judah.
2 He ruled in Jerusalem for three years. His mother was Maacah daughter of Abishalom.
3 Abijam did all the same sins his father had done. He did not love the LORD his God with all his heart, as his great-grandfather David had.
4 But for David's sake, the LORD his God gave him a lamp in Jerusalem. He raised up a son to follow him and made Jerusalem strong.
5 David had done what was right in the LORD's eyes. He had always obeyed the LORD all his life. He failed only in what he did to Uriah the Hittite.
6 There was war between the houses of Rehoboam and Jeroboam all the days of Abijam's life.
7 The rest of Abijam's acts and all he did are written in the Book of the History of the Kings of Judah. There was war between Abijam and Jeroboam.
8 Abijam died like his fathers before him. They buried him in the City of David. His son Asa became king in his place.
9 In year 20 of Jeroboam's rule over Israel, Asa became king of Judah.
10 He ruled in Jerusalem for forty-one years. His grandmother was Maacah daughter of Abishalom.
11 Asa did what was right in the LORD's eyes, as his father David had done.
12 He sent the male temple prostitutes away from the land. He removed all the idols his fathers had made.
13 He also removed his grandmother Maacah from her place as queen mother. She had made a hateful Asherah pole. Asa cut down the pole and burned it in the Kidron Valley.
14 The high places were not removed. But Asa's heart was fully devoted to the LORD all his days.
15 He brought into the house of the LORD the silver and gold and other things that he and his father had dedicated.
16 There was war between Asa and Baasha king of Israel all their days.
17 Baasha king of Israel went to war against Judah. He built strong walls around Ramah, to stop people from leaving or entering Asa's land.

18 So Asa took all the silver and gold that was left in the treasure rooms of the LORD's house and the king's palace. He gave it to his servants. He sent them with a message to Ben-hadad son of Tabrimmon, the son of Hezion, king of Aram, who ruled in Damascus.

19 He said, "Let there be a peace agreement between me and you, as between my father and your father. See, I have sent you a gift of silver and gold. Now break your peace agreement with Baasha king of Israel, so he will leave me alone."
20 Ben-hadad listened to King Asa. He sent his army commanders against the cities of Israel. They took Ijon, Dan, Abel-beth-maacah, and all the land of Naphtali, including the area of Chinnereth.
21 When Baasha heard this, he stopped building walls around Ramah. He went back to Tirzah.
22 Then King Asa called all the men of Judah, with no exceptions. They carried away the stones and timbers Baasha had used at Ramah. With these, King Asa built up Geba of Benjamin and Mizpah.
23 The rest of Asa's acts, all his strength, all he did, and the cities he built are written in the Book of the History of the Kings of Judah. But in his old age, his feet became diseased.
24 Asa died like his fathers before him. They buried him with his fathers in the city of his father David. His son Jehoshaphat became king in his place.
25 In the second year of Asa's rule over Judah, Nadab son of Jeroboam became king of Israel. He ruled for two years.
26 He did evil in the LORD's sight. He walked in the way of his father and in the sin he had made Israel commit.
27 Then Baasha son of Ahijah, of the house of Issachar, made a secret plan against Nadab. Baasha killed him at Gibbethon of the Philistines. Nadab and all Israel were attacking the city at the time.
28 In the third year of Asa's rule over Judah, Baasha killed Nadab. Baasha became king in his place.
29 As soon as Baasha became king, he killed the whole family of Jeroboam. He did not leave anyone alive. He destroyed them all, just as the LORD had said through His servant Ahijah the Shilonite.
30 This happened because of the sins Jeroboam had done and had made Israel commit. He had made the LORD, the God of Israel, angry.
31 The rest of Nadab's acts and all he did are written in the Book of the History of the Kings of Israel.
32 There was war between Asa and Baasha king of Israel all their days.
33 In the third year of Asa's rule over Judah, Baasha son of Ahijah became king of all Israel. He ruled in Tirzah for twenty-four years.
34 Baasha did evil in the LORD's sight. He walked in the way of Jeroboam and in the sin he had made Israel commit.

title: 1 Kings 16
sourceName: Lectio Easy Bible — AI 번역 — 1 Kings
paragraphs: 1 Then the LORD spoke to Jehu son of Hanani against Baasha. He said:
2 "I raised you up from the dust. I made you ruler over My people Israel. But you followed the ways of Jeroboam. You made My people Israel sin. You made Me angry by their sins.
3 So now I will destroy Baasha and his family. I will make your house like the house of Jeroboam son of Nebat.
4 Dogs will eat anyone from Baasha who dies in the city. Birds of the sky will eat anyone who dies in the field."
5 The rest of the acts of Baasha, and his strong deeds, are written in the Book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Israel.
6 Baasha died like his fathers and was buried in Tirzah. His son Elah became king in his place.

7 The LORD spoke through the prophet Jehu son of Hanani against Baasha and his family. Baasha had done much evil before the LORD. He made the LORD angry by the things he did. He became like the house of Jeroboam. He also killed the house of Jeroboam.
8 In the twenty-sixth year of Asa's reign over Judah, Elah son of Baasha became king of Israel. He reigned in Tirzah for two years.
9 Elah was in Tirzah drinking too much in the house of Arza, the manager of his house. His servant Zimri, the commander of half his chariots, made a secret plan against him.
10 So in the twenty-seventh year of Asa's reign over Judah, Zimri went in and killed Elah. Then Zimri became king in his place.
11 As soon as Zimri began to reign, he sat on the throne. He killed the whole family of Baasha. He did not leave a single male, family member or friend.
12 So Zimri destroyed the whole family of Baasha. This happened just as the LORD had spoken against Baasha through Jehu the prophet.
13 This happened because of all the sins of Baasha and his son Elah. They had sinned and made Israel sin. They made the LORD, the God of Israel, angry with their useless idols.
14 The rest of the acts of Elah, and all his strong deeds, are written in the Book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Israel.
15 In the twenty-seventh year of Asa's reign over Judah, Zimri reigned in Tirzah for seven days. The troops were camped against Gibbethon of the Philistines.
16 The people in the camp heard that Zimri had made a secret plan and had killed the king. So that very day in the camp, all Israel made Omri, the commander of the army, king over Israel.
17 Then Omri and all the Israelites marched up from Gibbethon. They surrounded Tirzah.
18 When Zimri saw that the city was taken, he went into the citadel of the royal palace. He burned it down on himself. So he died
19 because of the sins he had done. He did evil before the LORD. He followed the example of Jeroboam and his sin. Jeroboam had sinned and made Israel sin.
20 The rest of the acts of Zimri, and his betrayal, are written in the Book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Israel.
21 At that time the people of Israel were divided. Half followed Tibni son of Ginath as king. Half followed Omri.
22 But the people who followed Omri were stronger than the people who followed Tibni son of Ginath. So Tibni died, and Omri became king.
